title: Signal API Entry Point
description: The journey begins at the SignalController, which receives signals from external bots. It uses custom annotations like @RequireBotSignature for security.
languageLesson: Spring REST Controllers use annotations to map HTTP requests to methods. Custom annotations can be used with AOP for cross-cutting concerns like security.

title: Orchestrating the Use Case
description: CaptureSignalUseCase handles the coordination. It follows the Single Responsibility Principle, focusing only on the flow: validate, save, and publish.
languageLesson: The Application layer in Clean Architecture contains Use Cases that orchestrate Domain entities and call Ports. They should not contain direct infrastructure logic.

title: Core Domain Model and Ports
description: The Signal entity represents the core business object. Ports like SignalPublisherPort define contracts that the infrastructure must implement.
languageLesson: Dependency Inversion Principle: High-level modules (Domain) should not depend on low-level modules (Infrastructure). Both should depend on abstractions (Ports).

title: Infrastructure Adapters
description: JpaSignalRepositoryImpl and KafkaSignalProducer implement the domain ports, connecting the system to PostgreSQL and Kafka.
languageLesson: Adapters translate between domain calls and external system protocols. This keeps the domain logic independent of specific database or messaging technologies.

title: Real-time WebSocket Dispatch
description: Finally, SignalDispatchKafkaConsumer receives the signal from Kafka and uses ExecutorWebSocketHandler to broadcast it to connected bot executors.
languageLesson: Spring WebSocket provides support for full-duplex communication. Combined with Kafka, it enables real-time distribution of events across a distributed system.
